source: CIS IBM CLOUD FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V1.1.0
title: Ensure Web application firewall is set to ON in IBM Cloud Internet Services (CIS)

input_row.source: CIS IBM CLOUD FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V1.1.0
input_row.title: Ensure Web application firewall is set to ON in IBM Cloud Internet Services (CIS)
input_row.description: The Web Application Firewall (WAF) protects against ISO Layer-7 attacks, which can be some of the most tricky. What is a Web Application Firewall? A WAF helps protect web applications by filtering and monitoring HTTP traffic between a web application and the internet. A WAF is an OSI protocol Layer-7 defense in the OSI model, and it is not designed to defend against all types of attacks. Deploying a WAF in front of a web application is like placing a shield between the web application and the internet. A proxy server protects a client machine’s identity by using an intermediary (for outgoing traffic), but a WAF is a type of reverse-proxy that protects the server from exposure by having the client's traffic pass through the WAF before reaching the server (for incoming traffic). Key benefits of a CIS WAF The IBM Cloud™ Internet Services WAF is an easy way to set up, manage, and customize security rules to protect your web applications from common web threats. See the following list for key features: • Easy setup : The CIS WAF is part of our overall service, which takes just a few minutes to set up. After you redirect your DNS to us, you can switch on the WAF and set up the rules you need. • Detailed reporting See greater detail in the reporting, for example, threats blocked by rule/rule group.
input_row.rationale: A WAF or Web Application Firewall helps protect web applications by filtering and monitoring HTTP traffic between a web application and the Internet. Types of attacks WAF can prevent A WAF typically protects web applications from attacks such as cross-site forgery, cross-site-scripting (XSS), file inclusion, and SQL injection, among others. A WAF usually is part of a suite of tools, which together can create a holistic defense against a range of attack vectors. How a WAF works WAF is a type of reverse-proxy that protects the server from exposure by having the client requests pass through the WAF before reaching the server. It acts as shield placed between the web application and the internet. A WAF operates through a set of rules often called policies. These policies aim to protect against vulnerabilities in the application by filtering out malicious traffic. The value of a WAF comes from the speed and ease with which its policy modifications can be implemented, thereby allowing a faster response to varying attack vectors. For example, during a DDoS attack, rate limiting can be implemented by modifying WAF policies. Impact: A WAF is an OSI protocol Layer-7 defense in the OSI model, and it is not designed to defend against all types of attacks. When a WAF is enabled on Cloud Internet Services the TLS traffic is terminated at the proxy, unencrypted, inspected for any attacks, and then re-encrypted and forwarded on to the application. Another thing to consider with WAF is that there may be false positives based on the application patterns and clients may get blocked or challenged. This can be mitigated by tuning WAF.
input_row.audit: The Web Application Firewall setting can be audited using the following mechanisms IBM Cloud Console 1. Log in to IBM Cloud at https://cloud.ibm.com. 2. Click Menu icon --> Resource list 3. Select the Cloud Internet Service Instance 4. Select the domain that needs to be audited from the domains drop down 5. Choose the Overview from the left panel (this should be the default landing page) 6. Under Security section Web Application Firewall should show up as Enabled IBM Cloud CLI ibmcloud cis waf-setting <DNS_DOMAIN_ID> -i Instance-Name Displaying WAF setting of domain 'DNS_DOMAIN_ID' ... OK ID     waf Mode   On
input_row.remediation: The Web Application Firewall can be enabled using the following mechanisms IBM Cloud Console 1. Log in to IBM Cloud at https://cloud.ibm.com. 2. Click Menu icon --> Resource list 3. Select the Cloud Internet Service Instance 4. Select the domain that needs to be audited from the domains drop down 5. Choose the Security section from the left panel. 6. Click on the WAF tab in the Security panel. 7. Switch the toggle to On IBM Cloud CLI ibmcloud cis waf-setting-update <DNS_DOMAIN_ID> waf-enable -i Instance- Name Updating WAF setting of domain 'e476aba943b5d7d29b96135c78aa55c9' ... OK WAF setting was updated successfully. ID     waf Mode   On Default Value: The Default value for the Web Application Firewall is Off in Cloud Internet Services. References: 1. https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/cis?topic=cis-manage-your-ibm-cis-for-optimal- security#best-practice-activate-waf-safely 2. https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/cis?topic=cis-waf-q-and-a 3. https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/cis?topic=cis-cli-plugin-cis-cli#waf 4. https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/cis?topic=cis-cli-plugin-cis-cli#overview 5. https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/cis?topic=cis-getting-started Additional Information: The proxy mode for the service must be enabled for the traffic to pass through the Web Application Firewall.
input_row.impact: A WAF is an OSI protocol Layer-7 defense in the OSI model, and it is not designed to defend against all types of attacks. When a WAF is enabled on Cloud Internet Services the TLS traffic is terminated at the proxy, unencrypted, inspected for any attacks, and then re-encrypted and forwarded on to the application. Another thing to consider with WAF is that there may be false positives based on the application patterns and clients may get blocked or challenged. This can be mitigated by tuning WAF.
input_row.references: 1. https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/cis?topic=cis-manage-your-ibm-cis-for-optimal- security#best-practice-activate-waf-safely 2. https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/cis?topic=cis-waf-q-and-a 3. https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/cis?topic=cis-cli-plugin-cis-cli#waf 4. https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/cis?topic=cis-cli-plugin-cis-cli#overview 5. https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/cis?topic=cis-getting-started Additional Information: The proxy mode for the service must be enabled for the traffic to pass through the Web Application Firewall.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
This control can be audited using IBM Cloud CLI commands, which means it is feasible to automate the audit process using IBM Cloud APIs or the IBM Cloud CLI.

**PROGRAM NAME:** ibm_cis_domain_waf_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use the IBM Cloud CLI command `ibmcloud cis waf-setting <DNS_DOMAIN_ID> -i <Instance-Name>` to retrieve the WAF status for a specific DNS domain in the Cloud Internet Services instance.
- The program should enumerate all DNS domains under the relevant Cloud Internet Services instance(s).
- Check the configuration property "waf Mode" for each domain and validate that it is set to "On".
- Pass/Fail criteria:
  - **Pass**: The "waf Mode" is set to "On" for the domain.
  - **Fail**: The "waf Mode" is not set to "On" for the domain.
- This automation ensures that the Web Application Firewall setting is active to protect against potential web application attacks, per the CIS Benchmark recommendations.

**SOURCE:** CIS IBM CLOUD FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V1.1.0
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE
- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is correct. The IBM Cloud CLI provides the necessary commands to automate the verification of the Web Application Firewall (WAF) status for domains in IBM Cloud Internet Services. The use of IBM Cloud APIs and CLI is appropriate for this control.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8
- The automation details are mostly correct. The command `ibmcloud cis waf-setting <DNS_DOMAIN_ID> -i <Instance-Name>` is valid for retrieving the WAF status. However, the command should be verified for the exact syntax and options available in the latest IBM Cloud CLI version.
- The program name `ibm_cis_domain_waf_enabled` is somewhat descriptive but could be more specific to indicate that it checks the WAF status.
- The Pass/Fail criteria are clear and align with the CIS Benchmark requirements.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**
- The program name could be improved for clarity. A suggestion would be `ibm_cis_domain_waf_status_check` to more explicitly convey that the program checks the status of the WAF.
- Consider adding error handling and logging to the automation script to capture any issues encountered during execution, such as network errors or authentication failures.
- It would be beneficial to include a step to verify that the IBM Cloud CLI is authenticated and configured correctly before running the command.
- Explore the use of IBM Cloud SDKs for more complex automation scenarios or integration with other compliance tools.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH
- The assessment is well-founded, and the use of IBM Cloud CLI for this automated check is appropriate. The technical details are mostly accurate, and the approach aligns with IBM Cloud capabilities.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**
- Modify: Update the program name to `ibm_cis_domain_waf_status_check` for better clarity and descriptiveness.
- Ensure that the latest IBM Cloud CLI documentation is consulted to verify command syntax and options.
- Consider adding additional automation features such as error handling and logging for robustness.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**  
Both AI opinions correctly identified that the IBM Cloud CLI and APIs provide direct, scriptable access to the Web Application Firewall (WAF) status for domains managed by IBM Cloud Internet Services (CIS). The audit steps in the control explicitly mention CLI commands, confirming that automation is fully feasible. The IBM Cloud CLI exposes the necessary properties to programmatically check if WAF is enabled, making manual inspection unnecessary.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:**  
ibm_cis_domain_waf_enabled_status_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- IBM Cloud Resource/Service: IBM Cloud Internet Services (CIS) Domains
- API/CLI Method: IBM Cloud CLI command `ibmcloud cis waf-setting <DNS_DOMAIN_ID> -i <Instance-Name>` or equivalent API endpoint
- Properties to Check: The "waf Mode" or equivalent property in the returned configuration for each domain
- Pass Criteria: The "waf Mode" property is set to "On" (or "Enabled") for every domain in scope
- Fail Criteria: The "waf Mode" property is set to any value other than "On" (or "Enabled") for any domain in scope

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**  
Both AI opinions agreed on the automated approach. The only minor difference was in the suggested program name; I selected a name that is both descriptive and consistent with IBM Cloud naming conventions, clearly indicating the check's intent and scope.

**SOURCE:** CIS IBM CLOUD FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V1.1.0
